The team then prompted the model to prove this simplified formula, and GPT-5.2 produced a proof using a mathematical technique none of the researchers had considered. The work appears in a peer-reviewed preprint with institutional affiliations, establishing basic reproducibility standards.

## What This Signals for AI Capability

The episode illustrates a meaningful gap between AI performance on routine tasks and performance at research frontiers. Lupsasca noted that when GPT-5 launched, Twitter reception was lukewarm—observers noted the model showed no dramatic improvement at writing emails. But at the science frontier, capabilities had advanced substantially. GPT-5 could reproduce one of his published papers in 30 minutes; a year later, GPT-5.2 could generate genuinely novel results.

For enterprises and practitioners, this suggests the capability ceiling is higher than headline benchmarks indicate, particularly for specialized, reasoning-intensive work. The practical implication: domain experts pushing these models beyond standard use cases are discovering substantially different performance profiles. The finding also underscores that breakthrough capability in AI x science may depend less on raw model scale than on effective prompting strategies—the "priming" technique of warming up the model with simpler problems proved essential to unlocking the solution.
items.original_title: 🔬Doing Vibe Physics — Alex Lupsasca, OpenAI

items.headline: ASML Dismisses Startup Rivals as Chip Monopoly Widens
items.body:
ASML's new CEO Christophe Fouquet said this week that no competitor poses a meaningful threat to the Dutch company's stranglehold on advanced chip manufacturing equipment, even as startups backed by prominent venture capitalists claim they can break the monopoly. The remarks signal confidence bordering on indifference at a company whose machines are the irreplaceable bottleneck in global AI infrastructure—and underscore the structural moat protecting the world's most valuable European company.

## The Bottleneck Deepens

ASML manufactures the only machines capable of extreme ultraviolet lithography (EUV), the process required to print the microscopic patterns on silicon wafers that define the most advanced semiconductors. Each machine costs $200 million to $400 million, takes months to assemble, and involves hundreds of suppliers. The company's latest high-NA EUV systems command $350 million or more per unit. With Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, and Google alone committing over $600 billion to AI infrastructure spending this year, chip demand has created a supply crisis expected to persist for three to five years, according to Fouquet. ASML itself acknowledges the world will lack sufficient chips for years ahead.

Fouquet addressed criticism from TSMC about high-NA pricing by noting that while the new machines cost more upfront, they reduce per-wafer manufacturing costs by 20 to 30 percent on advanced layers. He suggested complaints about price reflect market immaturity, pointing to identical press coverage of earlier EUV generations that eventually became standard.

## Why Rivals Face Structural Barriers

Fouquet dismissed Substrate, a San Francisco startup valued over $1 billion and claiming to build a rival EUV machine, as operating in a realm of "wanting to have it and having it." He emphasized that lithography requires not just producing an image but doing so at scale, low cost, at high speed, and with nanometer accuracy. ASML spent 20 years solving the physics of EUV light generation alone—a problem it could only tackle because 80 percent of the foundational technology already existed from prior products. Substrate and other competitors starting from scratch face "enormous" challenges, Fouquet argued.

For enterprises purchasing or consuming advanced chips, the outlook remains constrained. ASML's practical monopoly, buttressed by decades of accumulated expertise and supply chain integration, shows no signs of eroding in the medium term. Startups and foreign attempts at reverse-engineering represent longer-term risks but pose no immediate threat to pricing power or supply control. Plan chip purchases and AI infrastructure investments assuming ASML equipment scarcity will persist through 2028 or beyond.
items.original_title: ASML CEO Christophe Fouquet on his company’s monopoly: no one is coming for us
